perturbation.original_text: The Distributor is an independent contractor and nothing contained in this agreement shall be deemed or interpreted to constitute the Distributor as a partner or employee of the Principal, nor shall either party have any authority to bind the other in any respect, it being understood and agreed that all orders submitted by the Distributor are subject to acceptance by Principal in its sole discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: The Distributor is an independent contractor; however, for the purposes of this agreement, the Distributor shall be treated as an employee of the Principal, and each party shall have the authority to bind the other in all respects, regardless of Principal's discretion on order acceptance.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the established definition of 'independent contractor' by asserting an employer-employee relationship for the purposes of the agreement. This introduces confusion about the true nature of the relationship and the liabilities of each party.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text redefines the independent contractor as an employee, directly contradicting the IRS definition and criteria for determining employment status, particularly concerning control and independence. The IRS provides specific guidelines and tests to differentiate between an independent contractor and an employee, and this clause directly violates that.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 3121 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 3121 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Wages For purposes of this chapter, the term “ wages ” means all remuneration for employment , including the cash value of all remuneration (including benefits) paid in any medium other than cash; except that such term shall not include— (1) in the case of the taxes imposed by sections 3101(a) and 3111(a) that part of the remuneration which, after remuneration (other than remuneration referred to in the succeeding paragraphs of this subsection) equal to the contribution and benefit base (as determined under section 230 of the Social Security Act ) with respect to employment has been paid to an individual by an employer during the calendar year with respect to which such contribution and benefit base is effective, is paid to such individual by such employer during such calendar year. If an employer (hereinafter referred to as successor employer) during any calendar year acquires substantially all the property used in a trade or business of another employer (hereinafter referred to as a predecessor), or used in a separate unit of a trade or business of a predecessor, and immediately after the acquisition employs in his trade or business an individual who immediately prior to the acquisition was employed in the trade or business of such predecessor, then, for the purpose of determining whether the successor employer has paid remuneration (other than remuneration referred to in the succeeding paragraphs of this subsection) with respect to employment equal to the contribution and benefit base (as determined under section 230 of the Social Security Act ) to such individual during such calendar year, any remuneration (other than remuneration referred to in the succeeding paragraphs of this subsection) with respect to employment p
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the concept of an independent contractor by stating the distributor shall be treated as an employee. This fundamentally alters the legal relationship, potentially conflicting with IRS guidelines concerning employment status, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets focus on the definition of 'wages' under 26 USC 3121, they implicitly support the idea that employment relationships have specific definitions and legal consequences, which are undermined by the changed text's attempt to redefine the distributor's status. The changed text gives each party the authority to bind the other, regardless of the Principal's discretion on order acceptance, which is contrary to the original text.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by the laws of the State of Texas without regard to principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by the Principal at the time of any dispute, irrespective of conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specifies that Texas law governs the contract. The changed text allows the Principal to select the governing law at the time of a dispute, which directly contradicts standard contract law principles regarding choice of law at the time of agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification undermines the principle of 'choice of law' at the contract's inception, allowing the Principal to retroactively select laws. This violates the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, which generally requires the chosen law to have a reasonable relationship to the contract or the parties. This section says parties should choose the law at the time of the agreement, not at the time of dispute.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original agreement by allowing the Principal to retroactively select governing laws at the time of a dispute, rather than at the contract's inception. This undermines the established legal principle of 'choice of law', as described in the Law Explanation. While there are no directly relevant scraped law snippets provided, the Law Explanation explicitly states that this approach violates the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187. The change directly contradicts the principle of determining the governing law at the time the agreement is made.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be in effect until March 18. 2021, unless sooner terminated by either party upon (30) days written notice, without cause.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be in effect indefinitely, terminable only at the Principal's sole discretion, without notice.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a fixed term and termination clause with a 30-day notice period. The modified text allows the Principal to terminate the agreement at any time, without notice, effectively negating the distributor's rights and investment. This creates an unenforceable agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification directly contradicts the UCC § 2-309, which requires reasonable notification before termination of a contract, particularly in a distributorship agreement. Eliminating the notice requirement is considered unconscionable and unenforceable under the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts UCC § 2-309, specifically subsection (3), which states that termination of a contract requires 'reasonable notification' and that dispensing with notification is invalid if unconscionable. The changed text allows termination 'at any time, without notice,' eliminating the distributor's rights as mentioned in the Perturbation Explanation. This contradicts the reasonable notification requirement of the UCC.
